title: Municipality Registration in the Netherlands
metaTitle: Municipality Registration Netherlands | BRP Registration & BSN Guide
subtitle: How to register with your local municipality, receive your BSN, and complete one of the most important steps after moving to the Netherlands.
description: Learn how to register with a municipality in the Netherlands, including required documents, deadlines, BRP registration, and how to receive your BSN.

image.alt: An expat woman reviewing municipality registration paperwork at a wooden desk with a laptop showing a registration checklist, stacks of documents labeled address registration, civil documents, and residence permit, and a passport. The desk overlooks a Dutch canal with traditional houses and a bridge.

heroCta.title: Plan your first steps after arrival
heroCta.supportingText: Use the First 90 Days Planner and Moving Checklist to map registration, BSN, banking, and housing setup.

quickAnswers.label: Who must register
quickAnswers.value: People living in the Netherlands longer than 4 months.

quickAnswers.label: What you receive
quickAnswers.value: A BSN number and BRP registration.

sections.heading: Introduction
body: Registering with your municipality (gemeente) is one of the first administrative steps after moving to the Netherlands.
If you plan to stay longer than four months, you must register your address with the municipality where you live.
This registration places you in the national BRP database, creates or confirms your BSN, and enables access to many government and financial services.

sections.heading: Why municipality registration matters
body: Without registering you cannot easily open a bank account, receive salary, take out Dutch health insurance, apply for DigiD, or access many government services.
Registration is the gateway to your BSN and to most practical setup steps after arrival.
bullets: Open a bank account
Receive salary
Take out Dutch health insurance
Apply for DigiD
Access many government services

sections.heading: Who must register
body: Registration is required if you moved to the Netherlands from abroad, you will stay longer than 4 months, and you have an address in a municipality.
bullets: Worker relocating for a job
Student enrolling at a university
Family joining a partner
Entrepreneur moving business operations

sections.heading: When to register
body: You must normally register within 5 days of arrival. However, municipalities may require appointments, and appointments may be booked weeks in advance.
Book the appointment before arriving if possible.

callout.title: Practical advice
callout.text: As soon as you know your address, check your municipality's website for how to book an appointment. In busy cities, slots can fill up quickly.

sections.heading: How the registration process works
body: The process typically follows these steps. The municipality may complete your registration the same day, or it may take a few days depending on the municipality.
steps: Book an appointment at your municipality
The municipality registers you in the BRP

sections.heading: BRP and BSN explained
body: BRP (Basisregistratie Personen) is the Dutch Personal Records Database containing resident information.
Your BSN (Burgerservicenummer, Citizen Service Number) is linked to your BRP record. When you register, you receive a BSN if you do not already have one.
bullets: Taxes — the tax authority identifies you with your BSN
Healthcare — insurers and care providers use it
Banking — opening an account typically requires a BSN
Employment — employers need it for payroll
DigiD — activation requires a BSN

callout.title: Short stays (less than 4 months)
callout.text: If you are staying less than 4 months, you may instead register in the RNI (Non-Resident Records Database) to obtain a BSN.

sections.heading: Documents required
body: Requirements vary by municipality, immigration status, and household. Typical documents include a valid passport or ID, proof of address (e.g. rental contract), birth certificate, marriage certificate if applicable, and for non-EU nationals a residence permit or visa.
Some documents may require legalisation, apostille, or certified translation. Check your municipality and the IND for the latest requirements.
bullets: Valid passport or identity document
Proof of address (rental contract or housing confirmation)
Birth certificate (in some situations)
Marriage certificate (if applicable)
Residence permit or visa (non-EU nationals)

links.label: Documents needed to move to the Netherlands

sections.heading: Municipality differences
body: Rules and processes can vary slightly between municipalities. Appointment requirements, waiting times, and document checks may differ.
bullets: Amsterdam — often high demand; book well in advance
Rotterdam — check online booking and required documents
Utrecht — similar process; confirm local requirements
Eindhoven — appointment system; document list on gemeente site

callout.title: Confirm with your municipality
callout.text: Always check your gemeente's official website for the latest appointment booking, document list, and opening hours.

sections.heading: Special cases
body: Different situations can affect how and when you register.
bullets: Registering a partner or family — each person may need to be registered; documents for each family member
Registering children — birth certificates and possibly other documents; check municipality
Registering without permanent address (briefadres) — some municipalities allow a correspondence address in specific cases
Re-registering if you previously lived in the Netherlands — you may already have a BSN; bring previous documents
Short-stay registration (RNI) — if staying less than 4 months, register in the RNI to obtain a BSN

links.label: Moving to the Netherlands with family

sections.heading: Costs and timeline
body: Municipality registration itself is generally free. Costs may arise from translations, document legalisation, transport, or delays if you need to rebook.
bullets: Registration at the municipality — usually no fee
Processing — same-day or within a few days depending on municipality

sections.heading: Official sources
body: For the latest rules and procedures, always refer to official government sources.

links.label: Government.nl – What to arrange when moving to the Netherlands
Netherlands Worldwide – What is a BSN?

exampleScenarios.title: Software engineer moving to Amsterdam for work
exampleScenarios.summary: Secure housing, book a municipality appointment as soon as you have an address, bring passport, rental contract, and any residence permit. Receive BSN at or shortly after the appointment, then open a bank account and arrange health insurance.

exampleScenarios.title: Student registering in Groningen
exampleScenarios.summary: Confirm with your university and municipality: proof of enrolment and address (e.g. rental contract). Book the appointment early; student housing often allows registration. BSN is needed for part-time work, bank account, and insurance.

exampleScenarios.title: Family registering in Rotterdam
exampleScenarios.summary: Each family member typically needs to register; bring passports, birth certificates, marriage certificate if applicable, proof of address, and residence permits. Appointment availability may vary; book for the whole household.

exampleScenarios.title: Freelancer registering while starting a business
exampleScenarios.summary: Register your address first to get your BSN; you will need it for KVK registration, tax number, and banking. Bring proof of address and identity; if you are from outside the EU, bring your residence permit or visa documents.

scenariosSectionIntro: How municipality registration fits into different relocation situations.

related.label: Open a bank account in the Netherlands

howToSteps.name: Book an appointment at your municipality
howToSteps.text: Contact or use the online system of the gemeente where you live to book a registration appointment.

howToSteps.name: Gather required documents
howToSteps.text: Prepare valid passport or ID, proof of address, and any other documents your municipality requires (e.g. birth certificate, residence permit).

howToSteps.name: Attend the appointment in person
howToSteps.text: Go to the municipal office with originals and any requested copies. The municipality will register you in the BRP.

howToSteps.name: Receive your BSN
howToSteps.text: You will receive or be told your BSN at or shortly after registration if you do not already have one.

faq.a: You must normally register within 5 days of arrival. In practice you need an appointment, so book as soon as you know your address—ideally before you arrive.

faq.q: What happens if I miss the deadline?
faq.a: You should still register as soon as possible. Late registration can in some cases lead to fines or complications. Contact your municipality if you have already missed the 5-day window.

faq.q: Do I get my BSN immediately?
faq.a: In many cases yes—at the appointment or shortly after. Some municipalities issue it on the spot; others send it by post or make it available online within a few days.

faq.q: Can I register without an address?
faq.a: You need an address in the municipality to register in the BRP. In specific situations (e.g. briefadres) some municipalities allow a correspondence address; check with your gemeente. For short stays under 4 months, you can use the RNI to get a BSN.

faq.q: Can my partner register at the same appointment?
faq.a: Many municipalities allow household members to be registered in one or linked appointments. Check when booking whether you can register together and what documents each person needs.

faq.q: What if I already have a BSN?
faq.a: If you previously lived in the Netherlands and have a BSN, you still need to register your new address when you move back. Your existing BSN will be linked to your new registration.

faq.q: What if I stay less than 4 months?
faq.a: If you are staying less than 4 months, you do not register in the BRP but can register in the RNI (Non-Resident Records Database) to obtain a BSN. See business.gov.nl and Netherlands Worldwide for RNI details.
